patterns.what_to_look_for: Cleartext credentials, tokens, session IDs, or PII (PAN/CVV/SSN/email) written into application or access logs because whole request/response objects, headers, or auth payloads are serialized without redaction.
source_indicators: logger.info(req.body) / console.log(password) / log.debug('user=' + user) dumping credential or PII fields
Logging full headers incl Authorization/Cookie: logger.info(JSON.stringify(req.headers))
Morgan/Winston/log4j/Serilog printing raw bodies; Rails filter_parameters or Django logging redaction not configured
Variables named card_number, cvv, ssn, access_token, refresh_token, set-cookie passed to a log sink
Exception loggers dumping context objects: logger.error(e, { user, request })
blackbox_indicators: Secrets/session tokens in URLs or query strings that land in access logs: ?token=, ?reset_token=, ?api_key=
Verbose error responses echoing request body alongside a log/correlation id
Reachable log artifacts: /logs/, app.log, debug.log, *.log returning 200 or directory listing
Stack-trace pages that include the submitted credential or token value
evidence_required: Log line or artifact showing the cleartext secret/PII
The source statement that emits it or a retrievable log file proving emission
Mapping of the leaked field to its sensitive class (credential / PII / PAN)
false_positive_checks: A redaction/scrubbing filter is applied (***, [FILTERED], filter_parameters, log masker)
Field is hashed/tokenized rather than raw
The 'token' is a non-sensitive correlation/trace id, not an auth secret
Sink is access-controlled with short retention (note but downgrade severity)
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send a request carrying a uniquely-tagged dummy credential/token, then check any reachable log endpoint or error response for that exact marker string.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as sensitive-data exposure: cite the exact field and log line, identify who can read the sink, and scale severity by secret type and log accessibility.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Untrusted input (username, User-Agent, headers, path) concatenated into log lines without CRLF stripping or encoding, enabling forged log entries and SIEM/log-parser poisoning (log injection).
source_indicators: User input interpolated into a log string: logger.info('login failed for ' + username) / log.warn(f'path={request.path}')
Logging raw User-Agent, Referer, or X-Forwarded-For values verbatim
No \n / \r stripping or escaping before the log write
Concatenated string passed to a structured logger instead of parameterized fields
Downstream ELK/log4j/Splunk ingest that treats each newline as a new event
blackbox_indicators: Reflected-input fields (login name, header) that also surface in an admin activity/audit view
Inputs containing %0d%0a or literal newlines accepted in username/header/query without rejection
Admin 'activity log' UI that renders raw user-controlled strings
evidence_required: An injected newline produces a second/forged log line or breaks log parsing
The raw, un-encoded sink (file, UI, or SIEM) showing the line split
Source statement with the unsanitized concatenation
false_positive_checks: Logger JSON-encodes/escapes control chars (structured logging with field params)
Input length/charset validation strips CR/LF before logging
Sink stores raw but always renders escaped and is not consumed by another parser
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Submit a benign value with an encoded newline plus a marker token in a logged field and check whether a reachable log or audit view shows a split or forged second line.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as log injection/forgery: show the forged line and a downstream consumer (SIEM/audit UI) to establish impact beyond cosmetic noise.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Security-relevant events (login success/failure, MFA, password/email change, role or permission grant, data export, impersonation, admin/config change, deletion) with no audit-log write, leaving no forensic trail.
source_indicators: Sensitive controllers/services with no audit / auditLog / logSecurityEvent call
Auth, role-change, or permission-toggle handlers lacking any logging
delete / export / impersonate endpoints that are silent
An audit helper exists in the codebase but is not invoked on the sensitive path
Email/2FA change handlers with no history-record write
blackbox_indicators: Account security/activity page shows no entry after a sensitive action
No email or notification sent on password or MFA change
Bulk export/download leaves no visible record
Account-takeover-relevant changes (email, 2FA) without any history entry
evidence_required: Perform the sensitive action and demonstrate absence of a corresponding audit entry
Name the security/compliance event class left uncovered
Source path showing the missing audit call
false_positive_checks: Logging happens async via middleware/event bus (verify before claiming a gap)
A separate audit service/DB captures it out-of-band
Event is recorded under a different name or category than expected
patterns.suggested_validation_task: With test accounts, perform a sensitive action (e.g. change email or role) and check the account's own security/activity log for a corresponding entry.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report missing audit coverage tied to a named event class and its detection/forensics impact; map to SOC2/PCI/HIPAA obligations where relevant.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Audit/security logs that authenticated or low-privilege users can read, modify, delete, or that are not append-only, undermining non-repudiation and incident response.
source_indicators: Audit records in a user-writable table where UPDATE/DELETE are allowed (no append-only/WORM)
Audit/log endpoints (/api/audit, /admin/logs) missing an authorization check
Log files written world-writable or inside the web root
A 'clear log' / 'purge history' action reachable by non-admin roles
No integrity protection (signing/hash-chain) where non-repudiation is required
blackbox_indicators: Log/audit endpoints return 200 to a normal user or unauthenticated request
Log files browsable/downloadable via web (*.log 200, directory listing)
Ability for a user to delete their own activity entries
IDOR on audit records: /audit?user_id= returns other users' entries
evidence_required: Unauthorized read/edit/delete of an audit record (low-priv or two-account proof)
Or a reachable log file retrieved via path
Source/route showing the missing authZ or the destructive operation
false_positive_checks: Endpoint enforces admin role server-side (verify, don't assume the UI hides it)
Logs are mirrored append-only to an external SIEM (tampering is local-only)
'Delete' only soft-hides while the original record is retained
patterns.suggested_validation_task: As a normal non-admin test user, request the audit/log endpoint and common *.log paths and confirm whether records are readable or modifiable.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as audit-integrity / broken access control on logs: emphasize non-repudiation and IR impact and state exactly who can tamper.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Debug/verbose logging left enabled in production emitting stack traces, SQL, env vars, connection strings, or tokens to HTTP responses, consoles, or shared log sinks.
source_indicators: DEBUG=true / app.debug=True / logging level=DEBUG in production config
console.log / print of config, env, DB DSN, or secret values
ORM SQL echo enabled (echo=True, log_statements=all)
Error handler returning the stack trace to the client
Third-party SDK debug logging that dumps full request bodies
blackbox_indicators: Error pages exposing stack traces, file paths, and framework versions
Debug panels reachable: ?debug=1, _profiler, Werkzeug interactive console
Verbose 500 responses exposing the query or DSN
Source maps or debug endpoints reachable in production
evidence_required: Response or log artifact containing the leaked secret/internal detail
Confirmation the host is production, not staging
The config flag or log level enabling verbose mode
false_positive_checks: Debug enabled only on a staging/non-prod host
Traces are logged server-side to a restricted sink, not returned to the client
Leaked value is a non-sensitive placeholder/sample
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Trigger a benign error (malformed param) and inspect the HTTP response body and headers for stack traces, DSNs, or interactive debug panels.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as information exposure via debug/verbose logging: cite the exact leaked artifact, confirm prod, and recommend disabling debug plus scrubbing.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Tokens, session IDs, PII, or full payloads forwarded to external monitoring/analytics (Sentry, Datadog, LogRocket, FullStory, GA) or captured in URLs that land in access/CDN/proxy logs.
source_indicators: Error trackers capturing request body/headers without scrubbing (Sentry.init with no beforeSend or sendDefaultPii: true)
Session-replay SDK (LogRocket/FullStory) with no input masking configured
Secrets passed in query strings to endpoints whose URLs are access-logged
Analytics events that include email, token, or PAN fields
Access-log format string capturing the full URL including the query
blackbox_indicators: Third-party telemetry/replay scripts loaded (sentry, logrocket, datadog, fullstory)
Sensitive values in URLs (?token=, ?email=, ?sig=) routed through CDN/proxy logs
Bearer tokens leaking cross-origin via the Referer header
Unmasked form inputs visible in session-replay capture
evidence_required: Outbound telemetry payload containing the sensitive field (devtools network capture)
Or a sensitive value present in a URL known to be access-logged
Config showing no scrubbing/masking applied before transmission
false_positive_checks: beforeSend / denyUrls / PII scrubbing or input masking is configured
Telemetry disabled in production or gated behind consent
patterns.suggested_validation_task: With devtools network capture open, perform an auth or PII action and inspect outbound requests to telemetry hosts for unmasked secrets or PII.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as sensitive-data exposure to third parties: name the vendor host and leaked field, and reference data-handling/consent obligations.
